name: Beliche
notes: Powerful, hollow beach break at the SW tip of Portugal. Picks up more swell than any nearby south coast spot. Fast, dumping barrels at mid-low tide. Gets big and messy in W/NW gales.

name: Telheiro
notes: Remote west-facing beach just north of Cabo de São Vicente. Consistent swell, few crowds. Rocky access track requires a high-clearance vehicle. Best at mid tide on NW groundswell.

name: Castelejo
notes: Sheltered by cliffs on either side, one of the more forgiving west coast Algarve beaches. Long walk down from the car park. Works on smaller swells when Beliche is maxing out.

name: Amado
notes: Wide, sandy beach popular with surf schools. Usually has a forgiving wave with multiple peaks. Gets crowded in summer. Consistent and user-friendly. Popular learner's spot.

name: Bordeira
notes: Vast, uncrowded beach flanked by dunes near the village of Carrapateira. River runs across the beach in winter. Multiple peaks to choose from, usually mellow enough for intermediates.

name: Arrifana
notes: Semi-sheltered bay with a right-hand point and a beach break. The point focuses swell and cleans it up. Better than nearby exposed beaches when the wind is NW and heavy. Watch the rocks.

name: Tonel
notes: South-facing beach inside Sagres Bay. Picks up S and SW swells that the west coast spots miss. Offshore in N/NE winds. Go-to option when the west coast is blown out by westerlies.

name: Mareta
notes: Town beach of Sagres, very sheltered. Only works on solid SW-S swells. Rarely crowded with surfers. The calmest south-facing option in Sagres when conditions allow.

name: Zavial
notes: East of Sagres on the south coast. Protected from NW winds by the headlands. Picks up more S swell than Mareta. Can have fun, consistent waves when the rest of the region is messy.

name: Barranco
notes: Small, sheltered south coast cove between Sagres and Luz. Often overlooked and uncrowded. Works on mid-size S swells. Good backup when westerlies hammer the exposed beaches.

name: Ribeira d'Ilhas
notes: World Surfing Reserve. Long right-hand point over rock. One of Europe's most consistent high-quality waves. Holds up to 8ft+. Best at mid tide on a clean NW groundswell.

name: Coxos
notes: Heavy, hollow right over sharp reef. One of the best waves in Europe on a big NW swell. Very fast, very consequential. Not for the faint-hearted. Expert surfers only in overhead+ surf.

name: Pedra Branca
notes: Right-hand reef between Coxos and Ribeira d'Ilhas. Works best on a big NW swell with SE wind. A step below Coxos in intensity but still a serious wave.

name: Supertubos
notes: WSL CT stop. One of the most powerful beach break barrels in Europe. Hollow, fast, punishing. The sandbanks can produce legitimate Pipeline-style pits. Closes out at 6ft+.

name: Nazaré
notes: World record big waves. The underwater canyon amplifies swells to 20m+. Tow-in territory when big. The beach break at smaller sizes is for experienced surfers only due to the shorebreak power.

name: Praia Grande
notes: Long beach north of Sintra, consistent NW swell. Fast beach break, can produce quality barrels when the sandbanks cooperate. Popular with Lisbon-based surfers.

name: Costa da Caparica
notes: 30km of beach south of Lisbon. Multiple peaks for all levels. Gets blown out easily by the strong afternoon NW sea breeze. Best in the morning. Very popular with Lisbon surfers.

name: Pipeline
notes: The most famous wave in the world. Ultra-hollow left over shallow reef. Third reef fires above 15ft. Unforgiving — the reef has ended careers. Deep offshore lineup requires strong paddling and commitment.

name: Sunset Beach
notes: Open-ocean big-wave reef. Multiple peaks across a wide lineup. Tricky to read — channels shift, peaks move with the tide. Demands big-wave paddling fitness. Best 8–15ft.

name: Waimea Bay
notes: Big-wave spot that only breaks properly above 15ft. The Eddie invitational held here. Huge, powerful right-hander with a terrifying shorebreak. Calm in summer, explosive Dec–Feb.

name: Trestles
notes: WSL CT venue. Consistent reef break, world-class when a solid SW groundswell hits. Offshore in N/NE Santa Ana winds. Beach access requires a ~1.5km walk from the parking area. No cars on the sand.

name: Mavericks
notes: Cold-water big-wave spot that only breaks above ~20ft. Deep-water reef creates massive, wedging rights. White shark territory. Paddle-in only. Hypothermia risk in the NorCal Pacific.

name: Uluwatu
notes: Iconic left-hander inside a cave at the SW tip of the Bukit. Six sections including The Racetrack and The Temple. Works all tide stages, best 4–8ft on SE trade winds (May–Oct). Very crowded.

name: Padang Padang
notes: Short, powerful barrel over shallow reef. Needs a solid 6ft+ swell to open up properly. When it's on it's one of the best barrels in Bali. Very shallow at low tide — not for the timid.

name: Keramas
notes: East-facing reef on the opposite coast from Uluwatu. Offshore in the NW wet-season winds (Oct–Apr). Fast, hollow right-hander. Opposite season to the west Bali spots.

name: G-Land
notes: Left-hand point in a protected national park. Three sections: Kongs, Money Trees, Speedies. Accessible only by boat or through a jungle camp. Peak season May–Oct. Crowded when it's good.

name: Desert Point
notes: Long, dreamy left-hander over a shallow shelf. When it lines up it's one of the longest barrels in the world — riders have been filmed in the tube for 30+ seconds. Fickle, needs SW swell and offshore trades.

name: Macaronis
notes: Perfect left-hander, one of the most user-friendly Mentawai waves. Long, roping tube with a forgiving takeoff for a reef break. One of the few high-performance Mentawai waves accessible to strong intermediates.

name: Lance's Right
notes: The benchmark Mentawai right — heavy, hollow, and relentless. Best 4–8ft. Shallow on the inside section. Classic Indo boat-trip destination. One of the most-photographed barrels in surfing.

name: La Gravière
notes: WSL CT stop. The deadliest beach break in Europe. Thick, heavy sandbars produce powerful, grinding barrels. Best in autumn (Sept–Nov) when Atlantic groundswells peak. Shallow, unforgiving.

name: Lacanau
notes: Pine-forest beach town north of Bordeaux. Consistent Atlantic swell, multiple peaks spread across a long beach. Less powerful than Hossegor, good for intermediates improving on beach break.

name: Lafitenia
notes: Long, peeling right-hand point in the French Basque Country. More forgiving than the Hossegor beach breaks. Connects sections on bigger swells. Beautiful setting above a sandy bay.

name: Mundaka
notes: The best left-hander in Europe. River-mouth wave with a funneling effect. Breaks over a sand/rock bottom. Fickle — needs N-NNW swell with offshore SE winds. The sandbars shift seasonally, affecting quality.

name: Zarautz
notes: Long beach town with consistent beach break. Hosts the Zarautz Pro. Works on NW-W swells with S winds. Good alternative when Mundaka is firing too hard for intermediates.

name: El Quemao
notes: Volcanic reef slab. Heavy, grinding left-hander that detonates over sharp lava. Needs a solid NW Atlantic swell. Not many sessions per year when it's truly firing. Cold water for the Canaries, thick wetsuit in winter.

name: El Confital
notes: Long right-hand point break on the northern tip of Gran Canaria. One of the longest rideable waves in the Canaries. Consistent N swell in winter. Can be sharky in the late evenings.

name: Anchor Point
notes: Long, walling right-hander in Morocco's surf mecca. Consistent NW Atlantic swell from Oct–Apr. Taghazout village is a well-established surf camp hub. Rides of 200m+ on a big day.

name: Jeffreys Bay
notes: The most perfect right-hand point break in the world. Eight sections that link up on a big SW Indian Ocean swell. WSL CT venue in July. Consistent May–Sept. Dolphins everywhere, occasional white shark.

name: Teahupoo
notes: The heaviest wave in the world. Breaks over an extremely shallow reef in less than 1m of water. The lip throws out and down simultaneously. Even small Chopes is heavy by global standards. Boat access only.

name: Cloudbreak
notes: World-class left off Tavarua island. Multiple sections from punchy to hollow depending on swell size and direction. Holds massive swell. SE trades keep it offshore most of the year. Boat access from the island resort.

name: Chicama
notes: Longest left-hand point break in the world — over 4km on a big day. Consistent SW Pacific swell. Waves are fun rather than heavy. The Humboldt Current keeps water ice cold year-round. Offshore in the SE trade winds.

name: Puerto Escondido
notes: Mexican Pipeline. Punishing, fast beach break barrel. Closes out at overhead+. Holds enormous S Pacific swells in summer (May–Sept). The underwater shelf focuses swell dramatically. Rips are lethal.

name: Snapper Rocks
notes: Start of the Gold Coast Superbank. World-class right-hander that links into Kirra on big E-SE swells. WSL CT opener. The sand pumping project has improved consistency. Dense crowd, competitive lineup.

name: Bells Beach
notes: Iconic Easter surfing tradition. Powerful SW Southern Ocean swell over a rock ledge. Three sections. Cold water, fickle — needs big, long-period SW groundswell. The rip bowl section can be massive.

name: Margaret River
notes: World-class right-hand reef (Surfers Point). Consistent SW Indian Ocean groundswell. The Box nearby is one of the heaviest slabs in Australia. WSL CT event. Cold by Australian standards in winter.

name: Fistral Beach
notes: UK's most famous surf beach. Consistent Atlantic swell, sheltered from the north. Hosts the English National Surf Championships. Multiple peaks for all levels. Cold, busy in summer.

name: Thurso East
notes: World-class cold-water slab. Heavy, hollow right over flat rock near the Arctic Circle. Peak season Oct–Mar when N Atlantic storms deliver. Water temps 5–8°C, full 5/4mm+ required. Remote, raw, magnificent.

name: Bundoran
notes: Ireland's surf capital. Right-hand reef break (The Peak) in the centre of town. Consistent Atlantic swell from Sept–Apr. The Tullan Strand beach break nearby is better for intermediates. Cold, but the craic is mighty.
